title: Lifecycle-Aware Components
description: Activity/Fragment lifecycle, LifecycleObserver, lifecycleScope, repeatOnLifecycle, and lifecycle-aware collection

references.title: Manuel Vivo — repeatOnLifecycle API Design Story

lesson:
Activity & Fragment Lifecycle States

Every Activity and Fragment passes through a finite set of lifecycle states. The Lifecycle library models these as an enum with transitions triggered by events:

INITIALIZED │ ON_CREATE │ ▼ CREATED │ ON_START │ ▼ STARTED │ ON_RESUME │ ▼ RESUMED ◄── fully visible, interactive │ ON_PAUSE │ ▼ STARTED ◄── visible but not focused │ ON_STOP │ ▼ CREATED ◄── not visible │ ON_DESTROY │ ▼ DESTROYED
States vs Events: States are nodes (CREATED, STARTED, RESUMED). Events are edges (ON_CREATE, ON_START, ON_RESUME). An observer reacts to events, and you check states to know where you are.

The Old Way: Imperative onResume/onPause

Before lifecycle-aware components, every resource that needed start/stop was manually wired in the Activity:

// The OLD imperative way — boilerplate and leak-prone class OldActivity : AppCompatActivity() { private lateinit var locationClient: FusedLocationClient private lateinit var sensorManager: SensorManager private lateinit var analyticsTracker: AnalyticsTracker private var mediaPlayer: MediaPlayer? = null override fun onResume() { super.onResume() locationClient.startUpdates() // register sensorManager.registerListener(this, accel, NORMAL) analyticsTracker.onScreenVisible() mediaPlayer?.start() } override fun onPause() { super.onPause() locationClient.stopUpdates() // unregister sensorManager.unregisterListener(this) analyticsTracker.onScreenHidden() mediaPlayer?.pause() } // Problems: // 1. Activity knows about EVERY component // 2. Easy to forget one unregister → MEMORY LEAK // 3. Order-dependent bugs (unregister before register completes) // 4. Adding a new component means editing Activity }
Real-world consequence: Forgetting to unregister a LocationListener in onPause means the GPS stays active after the user leaves — draining battery and leaking the Activity reference.

LifecycleObserver — The Declarative Pattern

With DefaultLifecycleObserver, each component manages its own lifecycle. The Activity just adds observers — it doesn't know the details:

// Self-contained component — manages its own start/stop class LocationObserver( private val context: Context, private val onLocation: (Location) -> Unit ) : DefaultLifecycleObserver { private val client = LocationServices.getFusedLocationProviderClient(context) override fun onStart(owner: LifecycleOwner) { client.requestLocationUpdates(request, callback, Looper.getMainLooper()) } override fun onStop(owner: LifecycleOwner) { client.removeLocationUpdates(callback) } } // Activity is now CLEAN — just adds observers class CleanActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) lifecycle.addObserver(LocationObserver(this) { loc -> updateMap(loc) }) lifecycle.addObserver(AnalyticsObserver(screenName = "home")) lifecycle.addObserver(SensorObserver(sensorManager)) // Each observer manages itself. Nothing to forget. } }
DefaultLifecycleObserver replaced the annotation-based @OnLifecycleEvent (deprecated). It provides typed methods: onCreate, onStart, onResume, onPause, onStop, onDestroy.

lifecycleScope — Auto-Cancelled Coroutines

lifecycleScope is a CoroutineScope tied to the Lifecycle. It uses SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Main.immediate and is automatically cancelled when the Lifecycle reaches DESTROYED:

// lifecycleScope — safe coroutine launching in Activities/Fragments class MyFragment : Fragment() { override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onViewCreated(view, savedInstanceState) viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch { val data = repository.fetchData() binding.textView.text = data.toString() // If Fragment is destroyed while fetching, // the coroutine is CANCELLED — no crash, no leak } } }
Fragment gotcha: Use viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope, NOT lifecycleScope (which is the Fragment's own lifecycle). The view can be destroyed and recreated while the Fragment lives — using the wrong scope causes crashes when accessing destroyed views.

repeatOnLifecycle — The Right Way to Collect Flows

This is the most important API in this module. repeatOnLifecycle launches a block when the lifecycle reaches a target state, cancels it when it drops below, and relaunches when it reaches the state again:

repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) Activity lifecycle: CREATED ──► STARTED ──► RESUMED ──► STARTED ──► CREATED ──► STARTED ──► RESUMED │ │ │ Block LAUNCHED Block CANCELLED Block RELAUNCHED (flow collecting) (flow cancelled!) (new collection) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Timeline: User opens app │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ collecting ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ User goes home │ │ ░░ CANCELLED ░░ │ User returns │ │ ▓▓▓▓ collecting ▓▓▓▓ │ ▓ = block is running (collecting flow) ░ = block is CANCELLED (not just suspended — fully stopped)
// The standard pattern for collecting flows in UI class MyFragment : Fragment() { override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onViewCreated(view, savedInstanceState) viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch { viewLifecycleOwner.repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) { // This block runs when STARTED, cancelled when below STARTED launch { viewModel.uiState.collect { state -> updateUi(state) } } launch { viewModel.events.collect { event -> handleEvent(event) } } } } } }
Key insight: repeatOnLifecycle is a suspend function that suspends the calling coroutine, then manages inner coroutines by launching/cancelling them as the lifecycle moves. It never returns until the lifecycle is DESTROYED.

Why launchWhenStarted Is Deprecated

The older launchWhenStarted / launchWhenResumed APIs suspend the coroutine when the lifecycle drops below the target state — but they do NOT cancel it. This is a critical difference:

launchWhenStarted { flow.collect { } } vs repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED) { flow.collect { } } ────────────────────────────────────── ───────────────────────────────────────────── App goes to background: ├─ launchWhenStarted: coroutine SUSPENDED ├─ repeatOnLifecycle: coroutine CANCELLED │ └─ Flow producer still ACTIVE in memory │ └─ Flow producer STOPPED, resources freed │ └─ Upstream keeps emitting (wasted CPU/memory) │ └─ No emissions, no wasted work │ └─ Buffered emissions pile up │ └─ Clean restart when foregrounded │ │ ├─ Result: memory pressure, wasted work ├─ Result: efficient, no waste App returns to foreground: ├─ launchWhenStarted: resumes, processes buffered items ├─ repeatOnLifecycle: fresh restart └─ Stale data may flash on screen └─ Always shows current data
Deprecation reason: launchWhenStarted keeps upstream Flow producers alive while suspended. A LocationFlow or SensorFlow would keep producing values in the background — wasting battery and memory. repeatOnLifecycle cancels the entire collection, stopping upstream producers.

STARTED vs RESUMED — When to Use Which

Both are valid target states for repeatOnLifecycle. The choice depends on what you're observing:

STARTEDRESUMED
Active whenVisible (including partially obscured)Fully visible AND focused
Inactive whenNot visible at allDialog overlay, multi-window unfocused, notification shade pulled
Use forUI updates, StateFlow collection, data loadingCamera preview, sensor reads, video playback
WhyUser can still see the UI behind a dialog — keep it updatedCamera/sensors should stop when Activity loses focus (save battery, avoid conflicts)
Rule of thumb: Use STARTED for most UI data collection. Use RESUMED only for resources that should stop when the Activity is not the focused foreground window (camera, microphone, accelerometer).

flowWithLifecycle Extension

If you only have a single Flow to collect, flowWithLifecycle is a convenient operator that wraps repeatOnLifecycle internally:

// Single flow — flowWithLifecycle is simpler syntax viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch { viewModel.uiState .flowWithLifecycle(viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycle, Lifecycle.State.STARTED) .collect { state -> updateUi(state) } } // Under the hood, flowWithLifecycle does this: fun <T> Flow<T>.flowWithLifecycle( lifecycle: Lifecycle, minActiveState: Lifecycle.State = Lifecycle.State.STARTED ): Flow<T> = callbackFlow { lifecycle.repeatOnLifecycle(minActiveState) { this@flowWithLifecycle.collect { send(it) } } close() }
Pitfall: Do NOT chain multiple flowWithLifecycle calls — each one creates its own repeatOnLifecycle block. For multiple flows, use a single repeatOnLifecycle with multiple launch blocks inside.

ProcessLifecycleOwner — App-Level Foreground/Background

ProcessLifecycleOwner provides a single Lifecycle for the entire application process. It reaches STARTED when any Activity is started, and drops to CREATED when no Activity is visible (app in background):

// Detect app foreground/background globally class MyApp : Application() { override fun onCreate() { super.onCreate() ProcessLifecycleOwner.get().lifecycle.addObserver( object : DefaultLifecycleObserver { override fun onStart(owner: LifecycleOwner) { Log.d("App", "App is in FOREGROUND") analytics.trackAppForeground() } override fun onStop(owner: LifecycleOwner) { Log.d("App", "App is in BACKGROUND") analytics.trackAppBackground() syncPendingData() } } ) } }
Note: ProcessLifecycleOwner never dispatches ON_DESTROY. The process Lifecycle only goes between CREATED (background) and RESUMED (foreground). Use it for analytics, socket connect/disconnect, or pausing background sync.

Side-by-Side: Old Imperative vs New Declarative

OLD: Imperative (Activity owns everything) NEW: Declarative (components own themselves) ──────────────────────────────────────── ────────────────────────────────────────── class MyActivity { class MyActivity { val loc = LocationClient() override fun onCreate() { val sensor = SensorManager() lifecycle.addObserver(LocationObserver()) val analytics = Analytics() lifecycle.addObserver(SensorObserver()) lifecycle.addObserver(AnalyticsObserver()) onResume() { } loc.start() } // Activity doesn't know start/stop details sensor.register() analytics.trackVisible() class LocationObserver : DefaultLifecycleObserver { } onStart() { client.requestUpdates() } onStop() { client.removeUpdates() } onPause() { } // Self-contained. Testable. Reusable. loc.stop() sensor.unregister() // Flow collection: analytics.trackHidden() lifecycleScope.launch { } repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED) { } viewModel.uiState.collect { updateUi(it) } } // Problems: coupling, forgetting } // unregister, Activity bloat // Problems: none. Automatic. Clean.

questions.title: repeatOnLifecycle Behavior
questions.description: What happens to the block inside repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED) when the user presses the Home button (Activity goes to background)?
options: The block is suspended and resumes when the user returns
The block is cancelled entirely — all child coroutines and upstream Flow producers stop. When the user returns, the block is relaunched from scratch
The block keeps running in the background
The block is cancelled and never relaunched
questions.explanation: repeatOnLifecycle cancels the block when the lifecycle drops below the target state. This is not a suspend — it's a full cancellation. Upstream Flow producers (location, sensors, network) are stopped, freeing resources. When the lifecycle re-enters STARTED, the block is launched fresh. This is the key advantage over the deprecated launchWhenStarted.
hints: Think about the difference between suspending and cancelling
What happens to a Flow producer when its collector is cancelled?

questions.title: launchWhenStarted vs repeatOnLifecycle
questions.description: Why was launchWhenStarted deprecated in favor of repeatOnLifecycle?
options: launchWhenStarted crashed on configuration changes
launchWhenStarted only suspends the coroutine when below STARTED — upstream Flow producers keep running, wasting CPU, memory, and battery. repeatOnLifecycle cancels entirely, stopping producers
launchWhenStarted does not work with StateFlow
launchWhenStarted is slower due to thread switching overhead
questions.explanation: When launchWhenStarted suspends, the collector pauses but the upstream producer (e.g., a LocationFlow) keeps emitting values. Those emissions are buffered in memory, wasting CPU cycles and battery. When the user returns, stale buffered data may flash on screen. repeatOnLifecycle solves this by cancelling the entire collection chain — stopping upstream producers and freeing all resources.
hints: Think about what happens to a Flow producer when the collector is suspended vs cancelled
Where do emitted values go if nobody is collecting?

questions.title: DefaultLifecycleObserver in Action
questions.description: What is the output sequence when this Activity is opened, partially obscured by a dialog, the dialog is dismissed, then the user presses Back to finish the Activity?
options: CREATE → START → RESUME → PAUSE → RESUME → PAUSE → STOP → DESTROY
CREATE → START → RESUME → STOP → START → RESUME → PAUSE → STOP → DESTROY
CREATE → START → RESUME → PAUSE → STOP → DESTROY
CREATE → RESUME → PAUSE → RESUME → PAUSE → DESTROY
questions.explanation: When the Activity opens: CREATE → START → RESUME. When a dialog partially obscures it, the Activity is paused but still started (visible), so only PAUSE fires. When the dialog is dismissed: RESUME. When the user presses Back: PAUSE → STOP → DESTROY. A dialog does NOT trigger onStop because the Activity is still visible behind it.
hints: A dialog overlay causes onPause but NOT onStop — the Activity is still visible
STARTED means visible, RESUMED means visible AND focused

questions.title: Unsafe Flow Collection
questions.description: This Fragment collects a Flow without lifecycle awareness. When the app goes to the background, the Flow keeps emitting and updating views — wasting resources and potentially crashing. Fix it to use the recommended pattern.

checks.message: Use repeatOnLifecycle to make collection lifecycle-aware
Use viewLifecycleOwner (not this or lifecycleScope directly) in Fragments
Use Lifecycle.State.STARTED for UI data collection

questions.explanation: The fix wraps the collection in repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED), which cancels the collection when the Fragment goes below STARTED (not visible). This stops the upstream Flow producer, saving battery and memory. Using viewLifecycleOwner ensures the scope is tied to the view lifecycle, not the Fragment lifecycle — preventing crashes from accessing destroyed views.
hints: You need two things: a lifecycle-aware collection API, and the correct lifecycle owner for Fragments
In Fragments, the view can be destroyed while the Fragment itself lives on

questions.title: STARTED vs RESUMED Target State
questions.description: You are building a video calling app. The camera preview should stop when the user pulls down the notification shade or a dialog appears. Which target state should you use?
options: Lifecycle.State.CREATED — to keep the camera running as long as possible
Lifecycle.State.STARTED — camera should run whenever the Activity is visible
Lifecycle.State.RESUMED — camera should stop when the Activity loses focus (dialog, notification shade)
Lifecycle.State.INITIALIZED — to start camera as early as possible
questions.explanation: Camera resources should be tied to RESUMED, not STARTED. When the user pulls down the notification shade, the Activity moves from RESUMED to STARTED (paused but visible). Using RESUMED as the target means the camera preview stops immediately — freeing the camera for other apps and saving battery. Regular UI data (text, lists) should use STARTED, but exclusive hardware resources (camera, microphone, sensors) should use RESUMED.
hints: Think about what STARTED vs RESUMED means for the Activity's focus
The camera is an exclusive resource — what happens if another app needs it?

questions.title: Multiple flowWithLifecycle Anti-Pattern
questions.description: This code collects two Flows using flowWithLifecycle, but the second Flow never collects. Find and fix the bug.

checks.message: Use repeatOnLifecycle with multiple launch blocks for multiple flows
Each flow collection needs its own launch block

questions.explanation: .collect { } is a terminal operator that suspends forever (it never returns as long as the Flow is active). So the first collect blocks the coroutine, and the second one never executes. The fix uses repeatOnLifecycle with separate launch blocks so both collections run concurrently. Each gets its own coroutine inside the lifecycle-managed scope.
hints: collect is a suspend function that never returns — what happens to the code below it?
How do you run two things concurrently in a coroutine?

questions.title: ProcessLifecycleOwner
options: When any Activity calls onStop()
When the last visible Activity in the app calls onStop() — meaning the entire app is now in the background
When the app process is killed by the system
Never — ProcessLifecycleOwner only dispatches ON_CREATE and ON_DESTROY
questions.explanation: ProcessLifecycleOwner tracks the aggregate lifecycle of all Activities. It dispatches ON_START when the first Activity becomes visible, and ON_STOP when the last Activity stops being visible (app goes to background). It never dispatches ON_DESTROY — the process lifecycle does not model process death. Use it for app-level foreground/background detection, not for individual Activity tracking.
hints: Process lifecycle is about the whole app, not individual screens
When is an app considered 'in the background'?

questions.title: repeatOnLifecycle Restart Behavior
questions.description: The user opens the app, goes to home screen (background), then returns. How many times does the Log.d line execute?
options: Once — the block only runs on first launch
Twice — the block is launched when STARTED, cancelled when backgrounded, then relaunched when the user returns
Three times — once for each lifecycle event (CREATE, START, RESUME)
Zero — repeatOnLifecycle suspends and the log never executes
questions.explanation: The block runs twice. First, when the Activity reaches STARTED (app opens). When the user goes to the home screen, the lifecycle drops below STARTED and the block is cancelled. When the user returns, the lifecycle re-enters STARTED and the block is relaunched from the top — meaning the Log.d executes again. This is the "repeat" in repeatOnLifecycle — it restarts on every re-entry to the target state.
hints: What does 'repeat' in the name suggest?
After cancellation, the block starts fresh — from the very first line
